points: The neuron is the structural and functional unit of the nervous system, comprising a perikaryon (cell body), dendrites for receiving impulses, and a single axon for impulse conduction.
Neurons are morphologically classified into multipolar (most common, e.g., motor neurons), bipolar (found in special senses like the retina), and pseudo-unipolar (found in dorsal root ganglia).
Neuroglia are non-excitable supporting cells categorized into CNS glia (astrocytes, oligodendrocytes, microglia, ependymal cells) and PNS glia (Schwann cells and satellite cells).
Astrocytes are the largest glial cells and contribute to the blood-brain barrier (BBB) via their perivascular feet, regulating the chemical environment of the CNS.

Myelination in the Central Nervous System is facilitated by oligodendrocytes, which can myelinate multiple axonal segments, whereas in the Peripheral Nervous System, it is performed by Schwann cells in a 1:1 ratio.

Peripheral nerves are organized into three connective tissue layers: the endoneurium (surrounding individual axons), the perineurium (surrounding fascicles), and the epineurium (outermost sheath of the nerve).

Synapses are specialized junctions for communication; chemical synapses utilize neurotransmitters stored in presynaptic vesicles, while electrical synapses allow direct ion flow through gap junctions.
Ganglia are collections of neuronal cell bodies outside the CNS; sensory ganglia contain pseudo-unipolar neurons, while autonomic ganglia contain multipolar motor neurons.
Gray matter consists primarily of neuronal cell bodies, dendrites, and unmyelinated axons, whereas white matter is composed mainly of myelinated axons and associated glia.

Axonal transport is bidirectional: anterograde transport (kinesin-mediated) moves proteins and organelles toward the terminal, while retrograde transport (dynein-mediated) moves materials toward the soma.
Microglia are the resident macrophages of the CNS, derived from mesodermal monocyte-macrophage lineage, and act as the primary immune defense within the brain and spinal cord.
The Nodes of Ranvier are periodic interruptions in the myelin sheath that facilitate saltatory conduction, significantly increasing the velocity of action potential propagation.

The vascular system is structurally divided into the macrocirculation (heart and large vessels) and microcirculation (arterioles, capillaries, and venules), primarily responsible for nutrient exchange and hemodynamics.

The histological architecture of blood vessels consists of three concentric layers: the tunica intima (endothelial lining), tunica media (smooth muscle and elastic fibers), and tunica adventitia (outer connective tissue sheath).

Elastic (conducting) arteries, such as the aorta and its major branches, contain high amounts of elastin in the tunica media to dampen the pressure pulse and maintain continuous blood flow during diastole.
Muscular (distributing) arteries possess a tunica media dominated by smooth muscle cells, allowing for regional blood flow regulation through sympathetic-mediated vasoconstriction and vasodilation.
Arterioles function as the primary resistance vessels of the systemic circulation, significantly contributing to the maintenance of peripheral vascular resistance and systemic blood pressure.

Capillaries are classified into three types based on endothelial continuity: continuous (e.g., muscle and CNS), fenestrated (e.g., renal glomeruli and endocrine glands), and sinusoidal/discontinuous (e.g., liver and spleen).

Anatomical end arteries are vessels that do not anastomose with neighbors (e.g., central artery of the retina), whereas functional end arteries have ineffective anastomoses that cannot sustain tissue during acute occlusion (e.g., coronary arteries).

Veins are characterized by a thinner tunica media, a wider lumen, and the presence of semilunar valves (folds of tunica intima) that ensure unidirectional flow toward the heart, particularly in the lower limbs.
A portal system exists when blood passes through two distinct capillary beds before returning to the heart, seen in the hepatic portal system and the hypothalamo-hypophyseal portal system.
Vasa vasorum are 'vessels of vessels' located within the tunica adventitia and outer media of large arteries and veins, providing nutritional support to layers too thick for luminal diffusion.

Arteriovenous anastomoses (AVAs) are direct communications between arterioles and venules that bypass capillary beds, playing a vital role in thermoregulation, particularly in the skin of the hands and feet.
Collateral circulation refers to the compensatory network of vessels that enlarge to maintain blood supply to an organ or limb when the primary vessel is obstructed by disease or injury.

The epidermis is a keratinized stratified squamous epithelium consisting of five distinct layers in thick skin: stratum basale, stratum spinosum, stratum granulosum, stratum lucidum, and stratum corneum.
The dermis is divided into a superficial papillary layer containing dermal papillae and a deeper, thicker reticular layer composed of dense irregular connective tissue providing tensile strength.
Melanocytes, derived from neural crest cells and located in the stratum basale, produce melanin to protect against ultraviolet radiation through melanosome transfer to keratinocytes.
Langerâs lines (cleavage lines) represent the predominant orientation of collagen fibers in the dermis; surgical incisions made parallel to these lines result in better healing and less scarring.
Sebaceous glands are holocrine glands usually associated with hair follicles that secrete sebum to lubricate the skin and hair, except on the palms and soles.
Eccrine sweat glands are simple tubular glands distributed widely across the body surface, playing a crucial role in thermoregulation through the secretion of a watery fluid.
Apocrine sweat glands are concentrated in axillary and anogenital regions, become active at puberty, and produce a thicker secretion that results in body odor upon bacterial decomposition.
The hypodermis or superficial fascia contains varying amounts of adipose tissue, serving as a primary site for fat storage, thermal insulation, and protection of deeper structures.
Specialized sensory receptors in the skin include Meissner's corpuscles for discriminative touch and Pacinian corpuscles for detecting deep pressure and high-frequency vibration.
Arteriovenous (AV) anastomoses in the dermis regulate body temperature by shunting blood away from or toward the superficial capillary beds in response to environmental changes.
The arrector pili are bundles of smooth muscle fibers attached to hair follicles; their contraction (stimulated by sympathetic nerves) causes hair erection and 'goosebumps'.
The skin functions as an endocrine organ by synthesizing Vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol) from 7-dehydrocholesterol when exposed to ultraviolet B (UVB) radiation.

Muscle tissue is classified into three histological types: skeletal (striated, voluntary), cardiac (striated, involuntary), and smooth (non-striated, involuntary), categorized by their control mechanisms and morphological appearance.
The structural organization of skeletal muscle includes the epimysium (surrounding the whole muscle), perimysium (surrounding fascicles), and endomysium (surrounding individual muscle fibers).
The sarcomere is the fundamental contractile unit of skeletal muscle, extending between two adjacent Z-lines and composed of interdigitating thick (myosin) and thin (actin) filaments.

Skeletal muscles are classified by fiber architecture into parallel (e.g., sartorius), fusiform (e.g., biceps brachii), and pennate typesâunipennate, bipennate, and multipennate (e.g., deltoid)âthe latter of which increases power but reduces range of motion.
A motor unit is defined as a single alpha-motor neuron and all the muscle fibers it innervates, representing the smallest functional unit of muscle contraction.
According to Hiltonâs Law, the nerve supplying a joint also supplies the muscles that move that joint and the skin over the insertions of those muscles.
Muscles act in functional groups: the agonist (prime mover) initiates movement, the antagonist opposes it, synergists assist the prime mover, and fixators stabilize the origin of the agonist.
Smooth muscle is categorized into single-unit (visceral) types, which act as a functional syncytium via gap junctions, and multi-unit types (e.g., ciliary muscle), where cells act independently.
Cardiac muscle fibers are characterized by intercalated discs containing desmosomes and gap junctions, which facilitate rapid, synchronized electrical conduction across the myocardium.
Skeletal muscle fibers are differentiated into Type I (slow-twitch, oxidative, red) for endurance and Type II (fast-twitch, glycolytic, white) for rapid, powerful contractions.

Muscular regeneration varies by type: smooth muscle cells retain mitotic activity; skeletal muscle relies on myogenic satellite cells for repair; cardiac muscle has virtually no regenerative capacity, healing instead by fibrosis.
Blood supply to skeletal muscle is highly vascularized, entering through the neurovascular hilum, with capillary density increasing significantly in response to aerobic training.

Joints are classified structurally into three main types: fibrous, cartilaginous, and synovial; and functionally as synarthrosis (immovable), amphiarthrosis (slightly movable), and diarthrosis (freely movable).

Fibrous joints are characterized by the absence of a joint cavity, with bones held together by dense connective tissue, including sutures (skull), syndesmoses (middle radio-ulnar), and gomphoses (dento-alveolar).
Primary cartilaginous joints (synchondroses) involve bones united by hyaline cartilage, typically serving as temporary growth plates that undergo synostosis after maturity.

Secondary cartilaginous joints (symphyses) occur in the midline of the body, where bones are capped by hyaline cartilage and joined by a fibrocartilaginous disc (e.g., intervertebral discs and pubic symphysis).
Synovial joints are defined by the presence of a joint cavity containing synovial fluid, a fibrous capsule, and an internal lining of synovial membrane covering all non-articular surfaces.

Articular cartilage in synovial joints is typically hyaline (except in the TMJ and sternoclavicular joint where it is fibrocartilage), is avascular, non-nervous, and relies on diffusion from synovial fluid for nutrition.

Uniaxial synovial joints include hinge (ginglymus) joints permitting flexion and extension (e.g., elbow) and pivot (trochoid) joints permitting rotation around a longitudinal axis (e.g., superior radio-ulnar joint).
Biaxial joints include condyloid and ellipsoid joints, which allow movement in two planes (flexion/extension and abduction/adduction), such as the wrist and metacarpophalangeal joints.
Multiaxial ball-and-socket (spheroid) joints, such as the shoulder and hip, provide the maximum range of motion, permitting movement in multiple axes and circumduction.
Saddle (sellar) joints feature reciprocally concavo-convex articular surfaces (e.g., first carpometacarpal joint), allowing for specialized movements like opposition.
Hiltonâs Law states that the nerve supplying a joint also supplies the muscles that move the joint and the skin over the insertions of those muscles, providing a basis for referred pain.
The blood supply to joints is derived from rich periarticular anastomoses, ensuring continuous perfusion even when specific vessels are compressed during joint movement.

Bone is a specialized form of connective tissue characterized by a mineralized extracellular matrix consisting of an organic framework of Type I collagen and an inorganic component of calcium hydroxyapatite.
Morphologically, long bones are divided into a diaphysis (shaft), epiphyses (ends), and a metaphysis, which is the highly vascular region between the diaphysis and the epiphyseal plate.
The metaphysis is clinically significant in pediatrics as it contains 'hairpin' capillary loops where blood flow slows down, making it a common site for hematogenous osteomyelitis.
The functional unit of compact bone is the Haversian System (osteon), comprising concentric lamellae surrounding a central canal that houses neurovascular structures.
Volkmannâs canals run perpendicular to the long axis of the bone, providing a communication pathway between Haversian canals and the periosteum.

Periosteum is a dual-layered membrane; the outer fibrous layer provides structural support, while the inner osteogenic (cambium) layer contains osteoprogenitor cells essential for appositional growth and fracture repair.

Bone ossification occurs via two primary mechanisms: intramembranous ossification (direct mineralization of mesenchyme, e.g., flat bones of skull) and endochondral ossification (replacement of a hyaline cartilage model, e.g., long bones).
The nutrient artery is the primary source of blood for the marrow and the inner two-thirds of the cortex, typically entering through a foramen that points away from the 'growing end' of the bone.
Pneumatic bones contain air-filled epithelial-lined cavities, such as the maxilla and ethmoid, which serve to decrease the weight of the skull and provide resonance to the voice.
Sesamoid bones, like the patella or pisiform, develop within tendons to reduce friction, alter the direction of muscle pull, and protect the tendon from excessive wear.
Bone marrow undergoes a physiological transition from red (hematopoietically active) marrow to yellow (fatty) marrow with age, starting primarily in the distal parts of long bones.

Bone remodeling is a lifelong process governed by Wolffâs Law, which states that bone density and architecture adapt to the mechanical stresses and loads placed upon it through the balanced action of osteoblasts and osteoclasts.
Cartilage is a specialized, avascular, and non-innervated connective tissue composed of chondrocytes housed in lacunae within an extensive extracellular matrix.
Nutrient supply and gaseous exchange occur solely via diffusion from the surrounding perichondrium or synovial fluid, limiting the metabolic rate and thickness of the tissue.
The perichondrium is a dense irregular connective tissue sheath that provides vascularity and new chondroblasts; notably, it is absent in articular cartilage and fibrocartilage.
Hyaline cartilage is the most prevalent type, characterized by a matrix of Type II collagen fibers and a 'glass-like' appearance, found in the respiratory tract and costal cartilages.
Articular cartilage is a specialized hyaline cartilage lacking a perichondrium, facilitating smooth joint movement while being susceptible to permanent damage due to poor regenerative capacity.
Elastic cartilage contains a dense network of branching elastic fibers in addition to Type II collagen, providing flexibility in structures such as the auricle, epiglottis, and auditory tube.

Fibrocartilage is the strongest variant, featuring thick bundles of Type I collagen and minimal ground substance, designed to resist compression and shear in intervertebral discs and the pubic symphysis.
Interstitial growth occurs when chondrocytes within lacunae divide mitotically to form isogenous groups, a process critical for lengthening long bones at the epiphyseal plates.
Appositional growth involves the differentiation of chondrogenic cells from the inner cellular layer of the perichondrium into new chondroblasts that deposit matrix on the surface.
The territorial matrix immediately surrounding lacunae is highly basophilic due to a high concentration of sulfated glycosaminoglycans (chondroitin and keratan sulfate).
Calcification of the extracellular matrix is a degenerative change commonly seen in aging hyaline cartilage, which impedes diffusion and leads to chondrocyte death.
Unlike bone, cartilage lacks a lymphatic drainage system and undergoes limited repair, often resulting in the formation of dense fibrous scar tissue rather than new cartilage following injury.
Anatomy is derived from the Greek word 'anatome', meaning 'to cut up,' and serves as the foundational science for understanding the structure and organization of the human body.
The standard anatomical position is defined as the body standing erect, facing forward, arms at the sides with palms facing anteriorly, and feet together pointing forward.
The median (midsagittal) plane is a vertical longitudinal plane that bisects the body into equal right and left halves.
The coronal (frontal) plane is oriented vertically and perpendicular to the median plane, dividing the body into anterior (ventral) and posterior (dorsal) segments.
The transverse (horizontal/axial) plane intersects the body at right angles to both the median and coronal planes, dividing it into superior (cranial) and inferior (caudal) parts.
Systemic anatomy is the approach that organizes the study of the body into functional groups, such as the skeletal, muscular, nervous, and circulatory systems.
Regional anatomy (topographical anatomy) focuses on the spatial relationships and organization of structures within specific body regions, such as the thorax or the upper limb.
The term 'proximal' indicates a position closer to the trunk or point of origin, whereas 'distal' refers to a position farther from the point of origin.
Surface anatomy involves the study of deeper structures through palpation or visualization of landmarks on the skin, bridging gross anatomy with clinical examination.
The skeletal system is divided into the axial skeleton (skull, vertebrae, ribs, and sternum) and the appendicular skeleton (limbs and girdles).
Clinical (applied) anatomy emphasizes the practical application of anatomical knowledge in medical diagnosis, surgical procedures, and physical therapy.
Standard terms of movement include flexion (decreasing the angle), extension (increasing the angle), abduction (moving away from the midline), and adduction (moving toward the midline).
